concerns.text: FR-004 mandates 'multivariate ANOVA on the selected model'. If the selected model is Random Forest or XGBoost (as per FR-003), ANOVA is mathematically inapplicable as these are non-parametric, non-linear ensembles without defined coefficients or residual sums of squares required for F-tests. This creates a circular/invalid validation where the spec demands a statistical test that cannot be performed on the chosen predictor.

The validation target (p-value < 0.05 for impurity impact) is being tested via ANOVA on the *same* dataset used to train the model (US-2). In materials science, using the training set for significance testing of the model's own predictors is a form of data leakage/circular validation. The p-value will reflect the model's fit to noise in the training set, not an independent physical truth. Validation must use a held-out test set or cross-validated permutation tests, not a single-shot ANOVA on the training data.

The assumption that 'impurity concentrations reported in weight % can be accurately converted to atomic %... without requiring complex phase-diagram corrections' is scientifically unsound for MgB2. Impurities often form secondary phases (e.g., MgO, B4C) or substitute into the lattice, altering the effective stoichiometry of the superconducting phase. Simple atomic weight conversion assumes a single-phase solid solution, which is often false in doped MgB2, leading to systematic errors in the predictor variable (concentration) that invalidate the regression coefficients.

The target R² ≥ 0.75 on a held-out test set for a heterogeneous experimental dataset (combining Materials Project and SuperCon) is likely an over-optimistic empirical claim. Experimental Tc data for MgB2 has high variance due to unmeasured microstructural factors (grain size, porosity) not captured in the proposed features. Setting a fixed high R² target as a success criterion risks forcing the model to overfit or the researcher to discard valid but noisy data points to meet the arbitrary threshold.
